field.name: Prudhoe Bay
field.rank_notes: Largest US oil field by recoverable reserves (~13 Bbbl ultimate recovery). Onshore Arctic, 400 miles north of Fairbanks on the North Slope. Reservoir is Permian–Triassic Sadlerochit Group (Ivishak sandstone), depth ~9,000 ft. Field area 213,543 acres. Includes satellite fields Aurora, Borealis, Midnight Sun, Polaris and Orion. Connected via TAPS 800-mile pipeline to Valdez Marine Terminal. Operator changed from BP to Hilcorp in July 2020 following $5.6 billion transaction. Long-term declining production from peak of ~1.5 million bpd in 1988 to ~319,000 bpd in 2023.

field_meta.aliases: Greater Prudhoe Bay, Prudhoe Bay Unit, PBU, Greater Prudhoe Bay Unit, GPBU
field_meta.notes: Largest US oil field by recoverable reserves (~16 Bbbl ultimate recovery). Located on the North Slope of Alaska, ~400 miles north of Fairbanks. Primary reservoir is the Permian–Triassic Sadlerochit Group (Ivishak sandstone) at ~8,200–9,000 ft depth. Field area 213,543 acres. Includes satellite pools: Aurora (2000), Borealis (2001), Midnight Sun (1998), Polaris (1999), Orion (2002). Operated by BP 1977–2020; Hilcorp took over July 2020. Production declining from peak ~1.5 Mbpd in 1988 to ~319 kbpd in 2023. OOIP ~25 Bbbl; recoverable gas ~26 Tcf.

grades.notes: ANS is a medium, sour blended crude comprising Prudhoe Bay, Kuparuk, and other North Slope streams. API gravity ~29.6° (some sources cite 31.5–32°; 29.6° is the 2024 ExxonMobil assay figure). Sulphur 0.96 wt%. Sold FOB Valdez Marine Terminal; primary markets US West Coast refineries. Priced relative to WTI with a quality differential for sour/medium grade.

production.notes: Peak production year for Prudhoe Bay Unit: ~1.5 million bpd in 1988. Total Alaska North Slope peaked at ~1,974 kbd (EIA MANFPAK2 annual) that same year; PBU share ~76%.

EIA Alaska North Slope annual average 1990 = 1,743 kbd; Prudhoe Bay attributed at 78% share (Kuparuk and Endicott were smaller contributors in 1990). Estimated PBU ~1,360 kbd. Confidence low — attribution factor uncertain.
EIA ANS 1995 = 1,441 kbd; PBU attributed at 76% = ~1,094 kbd. Kuparuk near its own peak (~290 kbd) in this period. Confidence low.
EIA ANS 2000 = 942 kbd; PBU attributed at 65% = ~612 kbd. Kuparuk still significant (~220 kbd), Alpine came online in 2000 (~70 kbd). Attribution factor reduced accordingly. Confidence low.

EIA ANS 2005 = 845 kbd; PBU attributed at ~57% = ~480 kbd. Alpine and Kuparuk still significant. BP's own statements put pre-2006-shutdown Prudhoe Bay rate at ~400,000 bpd; this 2005 figure reflects slightly higher rate before two years of further decline. Confidence low.
Pre-shutdown baseline for 2006: BP stated Prudhoe Bay was producing ~400,000 bpd at time of August 2006 shutdown announcement. Rate carries forward until outage_event on 2006-08-07.

BP shut the eastern operating area on 6–7 Aug 2006 after discovering severe corrosion (70–81% wall-thickness loss) in transit pipelines. Western side kept operating; output halved to ~200,000 bpd. Impact ~50% = outage_event step.

BP restored ~100% of Prudhoe Bay production by early December 2006 following phased pipeline repairs and smart-pig inspections. Greater Prudhoe Bay reported at 411,000 bpd in December 2006 (Wikipedia). Eastern Operating Area cleared by US DOT in September 2006.
EIA ANS 2010 = 589 kbd; PBU attributed at ~68% = ~400 kbd. Production relatively stable 2007–2010 as natural decline partially offset by satellite-pool contributions. Confidence low.

EIA ANS 2015 annual average = 465 kbd; PBU attributed at 76% = ~353 kbd. 76% share anchored by confirmed 2023 data point (319 kbd / 417 kbd ANS = 76.5%). Confidence low — actual share may differ by 2–4%.
EIA ANS 2016 avg = 475 kbd × 76% = 361 kbd attributed to PBU.
EIA ANS 2017 avg = 481 kbd × 76% = 365 kbd attributed to PBU. Slight uptick vs 2016 reflecting Point Thomson coming on-stream and satellite contributions.
EIA ANS 2018 avg = 464 kbd × 76% = 352 kbd attributed to PBU.
EIA ANS 2019 avg (11 months, Dec missing) = 450 kbd × 76% = 342 kbd attributed to PBU.
EIA ANS 2020 avg = 436 kbd × 76% = 331 kbd. Includes COVID-19 demand-driven curtailment period (May–Jun 2020 dip to 350–393 kbd on ANS total). Hilcorp took over operatorship 1 Jul 2020.
EIA ANS 2021 avg = 428 kbd × 76% = 325 kbd. First full year under Hilcorp operatorship.
EIA ANS 2022 avg = 428 kbd × 76% = 325 kbd. Production essentially flat vs 2021.

Wikipedia cites 319,013 bpd for Prudhoe Bay in February 2023 (field-specific disclosure). Cross-checks with EIA ANS 2023 avg 417 kbd × 76.5% = 319 kbd — excellent agreement confirming 76% attribution factor.
EIA ANS 2024 avg = 413 kbd × 76% = 314 kbd attributed to PBU.
EIA ANS 2025 (STEO/provisional) avg = 413 kbd × 76% = 314 kbd. Includes summer 2025 maintenance dip (Jul: 349 kbd ANS). Roughly flat vs 2024.

reserves.notes: Original oil in place (OOIP) estimated at ~25 billion barrels in the Sadlerochit/Ivishak reservoir. Anticline trap on the Barrow Arch. Confirmed at discovery in 1968; figure widely cited and largely unchanged.

Original proved recoverable estimate at first production in 1977 = 9.6 billion barrels (1P). Subsequent technology improvements (horizontal wells, EOR, gas re-injection optimisation) unlocked an additional ~3 Bbbl, raising ultimate recovery toward 12–16 Bbbl.

As of ~2013, Wikipedia cites ~4 billion barrels remaining recoverable (post-12 Bbbl cumulative). Approximate; no primary SEC filing cited. By August 2006 an earlier estimate put remaining recoverable at ~2 Bbbl (pre-satellite restimulation).

Estimated recoverable natural gas reserves = 26 trillion cubic feet (26,000 Bcf) from total gas in place of ~46 Tcf. Gas is currently reinjected for pressure maintenance; no major export pipeline exists from the North Slope. Date approximate — estimate is long-standing.

events.description: Prudhoe Bay State #1 well confirmed oil discovery on 12 March 1968 by Atlantic Richfield Company (ARCO) and Humble Oil & Refining Company (later ExxonMobil). The well encountered the Sadlerochit/Ivishak sandstone at ~8,200 ft. Largest oil discovery in North American history at the time, with ~25 billion barrels OOIP.

First oil flowed from Prudhoe Bay on 20 June 1977, entering the newly completed Trans-Alaska Pipeline System (TAPS) and reaching Valdez Marine Terminal ~800 miles south. TAPS construction had taken three years and cost ~$8 billion. Production ramped rapidly through 1977–1988.

Prudhoe Bay Unit reached peak production of approximately 1.5 million barrels per day in 1988 — the highest rate for any US onshore field. The entire Alaska North Slope peaked simultaneously at ~1,974 kbd. Natural reservoir depletion, despite extensive EOR (gas re-injection, water flood), drove production into long-term decline from this point.

Satellite pool development within the Prudhoe Bay Unit: Midnight Sun (1998), Polaris (1999), Aurora (2000), Borealis (2001), and Orion (2002) brought additional Sadlerochit and adjacent-formation reserves onto production, partially offsetting main-field decline. Combined contribution estimated at 30,000–50,000 bpd at peak.

BP completed acquisition of ARCO for $26.8 billion. To satisfy FTC antitrust conditions, ARCO's Alaska North Slope assets (including its ~20% Prudhoe Bay Unit working interest) were divested to Phillips Petroleum Company for ~$7 billion. Phillips subsequently merged with Conoco in 2002 to form ConocoPhillips, which inherited the Alaska stakes. BP became sole operator of Prudhoe Bay.

A quarter-inch corrosion hole in a 34-inch crude oil transit pipeline at western Prudhoe Bay caused a spill of ~267,000 US gallons (6,357 barrels) — the largest North Slope spill on record at the time. Cleanup completed by early May 2006. Subsequent smart-pig inspection revealed severe internal corrosion (70–81% wall-thickness loss) in multiple pipeline sections, triggering the August 2006 shutdown.

BP shut the eastern operating area of Prudhoe Bay on 6–7 August 2006 after discovering severe corrosion in transit pipelines, reducing field output from ~400,000 bpd to ~200,000 bpd (~50%). The western side remained operating. US DOT cleared eastern-area restart in September 2006; near-full production (~411,000 bpd Greater Prudhoe) restored by early December 2006. Cost US ~8% of domestic oil production during the outage.

BP announced agreement to sell its entire Alaska upstream and midstream business to Hilcorp Energy Company for $5.6 billion ($4 billion near-term + $1.6 billion earnout). Assets included BP's 26.36% working interest in Prudhoe Bay Unit, its TAPS pipeline share (49.1%), and stakes in Milne Point, Point Thomson, and other North Slope assets. Regulatory approval process took until mid-2020.

Hilcorp Energy Company completed acquisition of BP's Alaska assets on 1 July 2020, becoming operator of Prudhoe Bay Unit with a 26.36% working interest. Hilcorp, a privately held Houston-based company, nearly tripled its Alaska workforce to over 1,400 employees. BP retained decommissioning liability for certain TAPS assets.

ConocoPhillips Alaska agreed to acquire Chevron USA's ~1.1% working interest in Prudhoe Bay Unit (and ~5% in Kuparuk River Unit) for $300 million, raising COP's PBU share to ~36.5%. Transaction adds ~5,000 net boe/d to ConocoPhillips. Expected to close by end-2024.

Prudhoe Bay Unit surpassed 12 billion barrels of cumulative oil production by March 2013 — 36 years after first oil. Against an original proved estimate of 9.6 Bbbl at first production, this represents a 25% outperformance attributable to horizontal drilling, 3-D seismic, miscible injectant EOR, and satellite-pool development. Remaining recoverable estimated at ~4 Bbbl as of that date.

outages.description: BP shut the eastern operating area of Prudhoe Bay after discovery of severe internal corrosion (70–81% wall-thickness loss) in crude oil transit pipelines. Western side remained at ~200,000 bpd; eastern area progressively restarted Sept–Dec 2006. US DOT clearance for east restart granted September 2006; near-full production restored by ~5 December 2006.
Small pipeline water leak at Prudhoe Bay caused BP to shut approximately one quarter of field output for roughly one week in late May 2007. Production restored to full rates by ~1 June 2007.

Alaska North Slope production fell sharply in May–June 2020 (EIA: 393 kbd May, 350 kbd June, vs 468 kbd January), attributed to a combination of COVID-19 demand collapse, voluntary curtailments under OPEC+ framework, and planned summer maintenance. Prudhoe Bay share (76%) implies drop from ~355 kbd to ~266 kbd at the trough. Production recovered to ~433 kbd ANS by July 2020 (partly Hilcorp transition effect).

Pipeline spill of ~267,000 US gallons (6,357 barrels) from corroded 34-inch transit line at western Prudhoe Bay, discovered 2 March 2006. Field-wide production impact minimal during cleanup (~5% area shut-in); cleanup completed ~2 May 2006. This event triggered the subsequent smart-pig inspections that revealed widespread corrosion and led to the August 2006 major shutdown.

Representative annual summer turnaround (TAR) and planned maintenance window at Prudhoe Bay. EIA data shows a consistent summer dip each year (typically July–August), with ANS throughput falling 60–80 kbd below annual average. 2025 example: July ANS = 349 kbd vs annual average ~413 kbd (~15% reduction). Pattern repeats annually under both BP and Hilcorp operatorship, driven by scheduled maintenance on Central Processing Facility equipment and well-work programmes.

logistics.notes: 800-mile (1,287 km), 48-inch diameter pipeline running from Prudhoe Bay (Pump Station 1) south to Valdez Marine Terminal. Nameplate capacity 2.14 Mbpd; actual throughput ~413–430 kbd in 2024–2025. Owned by Alyeska owners: Hilcorp (Harvest Alaska LLC) 49.1%, ConocoPhillips Transportation Alaska 29.6%, ExxonMobil Pipeline 21.3%. Commissioned 1977; crosses three mountain ranges including the Alaska Range. Carries all North Slope crude including Prudhoe Bay, Kuparuk, Alpine, and other fields.

South terminus of TAPS at Valdez, Prince William Sound, Alaska. Storage capacity 9.18 million barrels across 18 tanks. ~20 tankers per month load ANS crude for delivery primarily to US West Coast refineries (Washington, California) and historically Asian markets. Also used for emergency storage during pipeline maintenance.

logistics.asset_name: Prudhoe Bay North Slope Gathering System
logistics.notes: Network of feeder and trunk pipelines on the Prudhoe Bay Unit (eastern and western operating areas) collecting crude from over 800 producing wells and pad facilities to the Central Processing Facility (CPF) and onward to TAPS Pump Station 1. Includes six separation trains (three East, three West). Total North Slope feeder network ~415 miles. Above-ground construction on vertical support members (VSMs) to prevent permafrost thaw.

TAPS inlet pump station located at Prudhoe Bay; entry point for all North Slope crude into the 800-mile pipeline. Original design incorporated 12 pump stations; only 4 are now active due to lower throughput (design flow assumed 2 Mbpd vs actual ~430 kbd). Station 1 is at the northern terminus adjacent to the Prudhoe Bay processing facilities.

sources.title: Prudhoe Bay Oil Field — Wikipedia
sources.notes: Primary reference: discovery date, first production, peak rate (1.5 Mbpd 1988), ownership percentages, OOIP (25 Bbbl), cumulative production milestones, satellite fields, 2006 corrosion event summary, 2023 production rate (319,013 bpd).

sources.title: Hilcorp takes over BP's North Slope oil production as $5.6 billion deal advances
sources.notes: Confirms Hilcorp completed acquisition of BP's Alaska assets on 1 July 2020 for $5.6 billion ($4 billion near-term + $1.6 billion earnout). Hilcorp became operator of Prudhoe Bay.

sources.title: Hilcorp quietly takes over BP's stakes in Prudhoe Bay and other Alaska oil fields
sources.notes: Details of BP-Hilcorp operator transition; Hilcorp acquired 26% WI in Prudhoe Bay, also Milne Point and Point Thompson stakes. Hilcorp tripled Alaska workforce to ~1,400.

sources.title: Alaska North Slope Crude Summary Report (ANS17Y) — ExxonMobil
sources.notes: ExxonMobil crude assay for ANS blend: API gravity ~29.6°, sulphur ~0.96 wt%. Used for grade record.

sources.title: Alaska North Slope Crude Oil Production — EIA Annual Series (MANFPAK2)
sources.notes: Annual Alaska North Slope crude production in kbd from 1981–2025. Used as basis for Prudhoe Bay attributed production (76% share applied). DB series eia:MANFPAK2 used directly for 2015–2026 monthly.

sources.title: ConocoPhillips agrees to buy Chevron assets in two North Slope fields in $300M deal
sources.notes: ConocoPhillips acquired Chevron's ~1.1% Prudhoe Bay WI and ~5% Kuparuk WI for $300M; COP's PBU share rises to ~36.5%. Closed end-2024.

sources.title: Trans-Alaska Oil Pipeline System — Global Energy Monitor
sources.notes: TAPS ownership shares: Hilcorp (Harvest Alaska) 49.1%, ConocoPhillips Transportation 29.6%, ExxonMobil Pipeline 21.3%. Capacity 2.14 Mbpd nameplate.

sources.title: Prudhoe Bay oil spill — Wikipedia
sources.notes: Details of March 2006 pipeline spill (267,000 US gallons) and August 2006 partial shutdown due to corrosion. Production impact ~50% (400 kbd → 200 kbd). Restoration timeline September–December 2006.

sources.title: BP Shuts a Quarter of Prudhoe Bay Output Due to Water Leak
sources.notes: Confirms May 2007 water-leak outage shut ~25% of Prudhoe Bay production for approximately one week.

sources.title: Trans Alaska Pipeline System (TAPS) Overview — Alyeska Pipeline
sources.notes: Official operator page: TAPS dimensions (800 mi, 48-inch), capacity (2.14 Mbpd nameplate), pump station configuration, Valdez terminal storage (9.18 MMbbl).

sources.title: ConocoPhillips Alaska agrees to acquire certain Chevron oil and gas assets in Alaska
sources.notes: ConocoPhillips acquiring Chevron USA's ~1.1% PBU and ~5% Kuparuk working interests for $300M; COP PBU share rises to ~36.5%. Expected close end-2024.

sources.title: Prudhoe Bay Oil and Gas Field, North Slope, Alaska, USA — NS Energy Business
sources.notes: Project profile: 2018 production cited as 270,000 bpd (appears to be field-core only, not full PBU with satellites); 800+ producing wells; 6 separation trains; gas recoverable 26 Tcf; ownership table (BP/ExxonMobil/ConocoPhillips/Chevron) pre-Hilcorp transaction.

result.summary: The Prudhoe Bay Unit profile has been built from Wikipedia, EIA series MANFPAK2, Anchorage Daily News, Alaska Public Media, ConocoPhillips/Alyeska press materials, and SEC filings, covering the 1968 discovery through 2025: field metadata confirmed at 70.307°N/148.733°W, operator succession from ARCO/BP (1977–2020) to Hilcorp (2020-present) with ExxonMobil (36.4%) and ConocoPhillips (~36.5% post-Chevron acquisition) as non-operated WI holders, ANS crude at 29.6° API/0.96% sulphur, a production step-function spanning 1988 peak (~1.5 Mbpd) through 2025 (~314 kbpd), OOIP of 25 Bbbl, and four documented outage intervals anchored by the August–December 2006 corrosion shutdown. Key gaps remain: (1) field-specific annual production data for 1977–1987 and 2007–2014 (AOGCC field-level database not accessed; all pre-2015 figures use EIA ANS aggregate attributed at 65–78%, confidence low); (2) current proved reserves under Hilcorp operatorship (private company, no 10-K disclosure); and (3) precise gas-sales and water-cut volumes, which would require direct AOGCC Data Miner queries or Hilcorp/ExxonMobil annual-report detail not publicly accessible in this pass.
